title: The Year 1000 and the Take-Off
bullets: Between 950 and 1250 the Medieval Warm Period ran northern Europe about half a degree to one degree Celsius warmer than baseline, lengthening growing seasons, letting wine grapes grow in southern England, and providing the climate floor for the entire High Medieval boom.

Four agricultural innovations — the heavy mouldboard plow, three-field rotation, the rigid horse collar, and the watermill — more than doubled European food output between roughly 800 and 1100, with the 1086 Domesday survey counting 5,624 watermills in England alone.
Europe's population roughly doubled between 1000 and 1300, from 35–40 million to 70–80 million — the most demographically expansive period in European history before the Industrial Revolution.
By 1300 Paris had perhaps 200,000 residents, Venice and Milan around 150,000, Florence and Genoa around 100,000 — the largest western European cities since Rome's fourth-century population peak.

The high-medieval city was not a Roman survival but a chartered self-governing commune, with the famous formula that 'city air makes you free' — a serf who lived in a chartered city for a year and a day was no longer a serf.

While western Europe was catching up, the Song dynasty economy in 1100 was probably four to five times the size of all western Europe combined — printed books in millions of copies, paper money, water-powered industry, gunpowder weapons.

Feudalism — the layered system of land grants for military service formalized in lord-vassal oaths — was the political and economic structure of much of western Europe between roughly 900 and 1300, with the manor as its basic working unit.
subtitle: agricultural revolution, demographic boom, and the urban revival

title: Pope vs. Emperor
bullets: In late January 1077 the excommunicated Holy Roman Emperor Henry IV stood barefoot in the snow for three days outside the gate of Canossa waiting for Pope Gregory VII to lift his excommunication — the iconic image of papal supremacy over kings.

Pope Gregory VII's Dictatus Papae of 1075 made twenty-seven sweeping claims of papal authority including the right to depose emperors and to release subjects from oaths of loyalty to a wrongdoing king.

The Investiture Controversy ran from 1075 to 1122 over who could appoint bishops — king or pope — and was finally compromised at the Concordat of Worms, which separated the spiritual investiture (by the pope) from the temporal investiture (by the king).

The 1059 papal bull In Nomine Domini took the election of the pope itself out of the hands of the Roman aristocracy and the German emperor and gave it exclusively to the College of Cardinals — the papal electors who still choose the pope today.

Cluniac monastic reform supplied the institutional engine: the abbey of Cluny in Burgundy, founded 910, eventually oversaw more than a thousand daughter houses across western Europe and produced the reform-minded popes of the eleventh century.

The Great Schism of 1054 — mutual excommunications exchanged between Cardinal Humbert and Patriarch Michael Cerularius in the Hagia Sophia on July 16 — formalized the split between Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches that has not been healed since.

By 1100 the medieval papacy had its own electoral process, legal system, court system, diplomatic corps, and international taxation — a parallel sovereignty to the kings rather than subordinate to them, one of the structural inventions of the High Middle Ages.
subtitle: Gregory VII, Canossa, and the rise of the medieval Church as a political institution

title: The Crusades
bullets: Pope Urban II preached the First Crusade at Clermont on November 27, 1095, and crusaders captured Jerusalem on July 15, 1099, slaughtering most of the city's Muslim and Jewish inhabitants — modern estimates of the dead run from 3,000 to 30,000.

The People's Crusade of spring 1096 — perhaps 40,000 mostly unarmed peasants under Peter the Hermit — massacred the Jewish communities of Speyer, Worms, Mainz, and Cologne en route, then was almost entirely massacred by Seljuk Turks at Civetot.

Saladin retook Jerusalem on October 2, 1187, after destroying the Crusader field army at Hattin — and conspicuously protected Christians and the Holy Sepulchre, the opposite of crusader behavior in 1099.

The Third Crusade under Richard the Lionheart, Philip II, and Frederick Barbarossa won only a truce in 1192 that left Jerusalem in Saladin's hands while granting Christian pilgrims access to the city.

The Fourth Crusade sacked Christian Constantinople on April 13, 1204, despite Pope Innocent III's explicit prohibition, looting the Hagia Sophia and breaking the Byzantine Empire for fifty-seven years — a wound the Greek-Catholic schism never healed.

Four Crusader states — the Kingdom of Jerusalem, the Principality of Antioch, the County of Edessa, and the County of Tripoli — survived in increasingly reduced form until the fall of Acre to the Mamluks on May 18, 1291.

The cultural traffic between the Crusader states and the surrounding Muslim world ran overwhelmingly Islamic-to-European: Damascus and Cordoba had public hospitals, paved streets, and pharmacies that Paris in 1100 did not.

The Children's Crusade is largely a fifteenth-century legend; two unrelated 1212 movements led by a French shepherd boy and a German boy named Nicholas were merged into the legendary expedition of children sold into slavery.
subtitle: Urban II, the First Crusade, the Crusader states, Saladin, and the catastrophe of 1204

title: The Commercial Revolution
bullets: Six annual Champagne fairs in northern France ran continuously around the year between 1150 and 1300, providing the principal commercial meeting point of medieval Europe and developing the lex mercatoria, the merchant law that became a foundation of European commercial law.

Lübeck and Hamburg signed a mutual defense agreement in 1241 that grew into the Hanseatic League — more than a hundred north German and Baltic cities with major trading posts at Bergen, London, Bruges, and Novgorod.

Florentine banking houses — the Bardi, Peruzzi, Acciaiuoli, and later the Medici — invented bills of exchange, double-entry bookkeeping, and the international branch banking that financed the kings and popes of Europe.

The bill of exchange let Italian bankers earn interest on currency conversions while technically not violating canon law's usury ban — the great workaround that built the entire international medieval financial system.
The first European gold coin in regular international circulation since the fall of Rome was the florin, minted in Florence in 1252.

The Italian commercial innovations were largely adapted from older Islamic instruments: the suftaja became the bill of exchange, the mudaraba partnership informed the Venetian commenda, and the eleventh-century Cairo Geniza documents show Jewish merchants running international trade with India and Yemen on similar arrangements.

Founded in 1119 to protect pilgrims, the Knights Templar grew into one of the wealthiest organizations in Latin Christendom, pioneered international banking through their preceptory network, and were destroyed by Philip IV of France on October 13, 1307.

The Song dynasty had circulated paper money since 1024 — Western Europe would not have widely circulating paper money until the eighteenth century, a measure of how far Latin Europe still trailed China in commercial sophistication.
subtitle: Champagne fairs, the Hanseatic League, and the Italian banking revolution

title: The Twelfth-Century Renaissance
bullets: Translators at Toledo, Sicily, and Antioch worked Arabic and Greek manuscripts into Latin between 1080 and 1200, returning Aristotle, Euclid, Ptolemy, Galen, Avicenna, and Averroes to the Latin West after a six-hundred-year absence.
Gerard of Cremona produced more than seventy translations at Toledo, including Ptolemy's Almagest, Avicenna's medical canon, and Aristotle's Physics and On the Heavens.

Around 1080 a complete manuscript of Justinian's Corpus Juris Civilis was rediscovered in northern Italy and brought to Bologna, where the lawyer Irnerius built the school of legal scholarship that would become the University of Bologna.

Anselm of Canterbury formulated the ontological argument for God's existence in the Proslogion around 1078 — the most influential single piece of medieval philosophy and still a live question in modern philosophy of religion.

Peter Abelard fell in love with the brilliant young Heloise in Paris around 1117, fathered her child, was castrated by her uncle's hired men, and lived out his life in monastic exile while their letters became one of the great romantic correspondences in European literature.

Bernard of Clairvaux, Cistercian abbot from 1115, founded sixty-eight Cistercian monasteries, preached the Second Crusade, and was the most influential churchman of his generation not to wear the papal tiara.

Hildegard of Bingen wrote theological visions, a treatise on natural medicine, the morality play Ordo Virtutum, the largest body of medieval music by a single composer, and corresponded with popes, emperors, and Bernard of Clairvaux from her abbey at Rupertsberg.

The Christian-Aristotelian synthesis that Aquinas would attempt had two parallel projects: Averroes attempting it from the Islamic side at Cordoba, and Maimonides from the Jewish side at Cairo — and the three thinkers were reading each other across linguistic and religious boundaries.

Beginning around 1100 the troubadours of southern France composed Occitan-language lyric poetry around fin'amor — the convention of refined, often unrequited love between knight and lady that reshaped European literature for centuries.
subtitle: Toledo translations, Aristotle returns, Roman law at Bologna, Abelard, Bernard, and Hildegard

title: Cathedrals and the Gothic Revolution
bullets: Abbot Suger began rebuilding the choir of Saint-Denis outside Paris in 1140 with pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and large stained-glass windows — the conventional birth of Gothic architecture and the new style that would cover northern Europe within a century.

Three structural innovations defined Gothic: the pointed arch let vaults reach 100, 130, 150 feet; the ribbed vault concentrated weight onto piers so walls could be hollowed out; the flying buttress (Notre-Dame de Paris around 1180) transferred outward thrust to free-standing piers.

Chartres Cathedral was rebuilt with revolutionary High Gothic engineering after the 1194 fire, completed in just twenty-six years, and contains 167 windows including the deep blue glass that scholars still cannot fully reproduce.

King Louis IX built Sainte-Chapelle in Paris between 1242 and 1248 to house his Crown of Thorns relic, purchased from the Latin Emperor of Constantinople in 1238 for what was reportedly more than half a year's French royal revenue.

Beauvais Cathedral pushed Gothic past its limits — the choir vault was raised to 153 feet, the highest in any Gothic cathedral, and partially collapsed in 1284; the rest of the cathedral was never built.

Construction of Cologne Cathedral began in 1248 to house the relics of the Three Magi, was abandoned in the sixteenth century half-finished, and was completed in the nineteenth century from the rediscovered original medieval plans.

The medieval cathedrals were not built by anonymous communal volunteer labor but by professional specialist guilds paid in cash for skilled work, on contracts negotiated with master masons who were the international rock stars of medieval applied science.

The Gothic cathedral was a comprehensive theological program rendered in stone, glass, and sculpture — west portal Last Judgment, transept Old and New Testaments, stained glass running the biblical narrative from Genesis to Apocalypse for the largely illiterate congregation.
subtitle: Saint-Denis 1140, Chartres, Notre-Dame, the structural innovations, and Sainte-Chapelle

title: Universities and Scholasticism
bullets: The University of Bologna, conventionally dated to 1088, was the first studium generale in Europe — a guild of students who hired their own professors, fined them for showing up late, and founded the institutional model of the medieval university.
By 1300 the major university map of Latin Europe was set: Bologna for law, Paris for theology, Oxford and Cambridge for the liberal arts, Salamanca for Iberian scholarship, Salerno for medicine.

The medieval curriculum ran the trivium (grammar, logic, rhetoric — the verbal arts) and the quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music — the mathematical arts) before the higher faculties of theology, law, and medicine.

The Dominicans (founded 1216 by Dominic of Caleruega) and the Franciscans (founded 1209 by Francis of Assisi) — the two great mendicant orders — relocated the most ambitious preachers and theologians from rural monasteries into the new urban universities.

Thomas Aquinas synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology in the Summa Theologiae (begun 1265) — the masterwork of high medieval scholasticism and the official philosophical framework of Catholic theology to this day.

The Christian-Aristotelian synthesis Aquinas was building had Islamic and Jewish parallels in exactly the same generation — Averroes from the Islamic side, Maimonides from the Jewish side — and the three thinkers were reading each other.

The medieval universities were not 'open to all' — they were almost entirely male, socially restricted by Latin literacy, and recurrently in conflict with the surrounding city populations in the famous town-and-gown brawls.

The university created the international academic credential — a master's degree from Paris let the holder teach anywhere in Christendom — a small institutional invention that turned out to be very consequential.
subtitle: Bologna, Paris, Oxford, Salamanca, the mendicant orders, and Thomas Aquinas

title: Magna Carta to Mongol Shock
bullets: Forced on King John of England by rebellious barons at Runnymede on June 15, 1215, Magna Carta established that the king was bound by law, that no free man could be imprisoned without lawful judgment, and that taxation required consent — the foundational text of the English constitutional tradition.

Simon de Montfort summoned a parliament in January 1265 that for the first time included representatives of the shires AND the boroughs alongside the magnates and clergy — the institutional origin of the House of Commons.
The Cortes of León in 1188 was the first European assembly to include burghers from the towns, predating Simon de Montfort's parliament by seventy-seven years.

Pope Innocent III's Fourth Lateran Council of November 1215 issued seventy decrees that defined transubstantiation, required annual confession, mandated identifying clothing for Jews and Muslims, and codified medieval Catholic doctrine for the next three centuries.

The Christian victory at Las Navas de Tolosa on July 16, 1212, broke Almohad power in Iberia and opened the Reconquista's biggest century: Córdoba fell to Ferdinand III in 1236, Seville in 1248, and by 1250 four-fifths of the peninsula was under Christian rule.

On April 9, 1241, a Mongol detachment under Baidar and Kadan annihilated the Polish chivalry at Legnica; two days later Batu and Subutai destroyed the Hungarian royal army at Mohi — two complete victories two days apart.

The Mongol withdrawal from central Europe in spring 1242 was an accident of imperial succession — Great Khan Ögedei had died in December 1241 — not a Christian military victory; there was nothing in 1241 between the Mongols and the Atlantic that could have stopped them.

Marco Polo traveled overland to the court of Kublai Khan in 1271, spent seventeen years in China, and on his return dictated The Travels of Marco Polo while imprisoned in Genoa — the most influential European description of Asia for the next two centuries.

By 1300 the institutional inventory of western Europe — chartered town, international banking corporation, corporate university, constitutional charter, representative parliament, Gothic cathedral — was the recognizable ancestor of the modern western political and intellectual tradition.
subtitle: Magna Carta, Innocent III, the Reconquista's high tide, and the Mongol shock at Mohi 1241
